Name: tool_use_dense_reward_design
Title: Dense Reward Shaping for Tool-Use RL: From Binary Success to Sub-Goal Credit
Short Hypothesis: Replacing the current binary success/failure reward for tool-use tasks with a dense reward that gives partial credit for correct tool selection, valid argument formatting, and partial execution will reduce ZVF saturation and enable meaningful GRPO learning on tool-use benchmarks.
Related Work: Reward shaping in RL (Ng et al., 1999) and process reward models (Lightman et al., 2023) show that intermediate credit accelerates learning. Current tool-use implementations (Qin et al., 2023; Patil et al., 2023) typically use outcome-only rewards, which leads to sparse gradients and high ZVF in GRPO training.
Abstract: Tool-use RL fine-tuning currently yields zero-reward outcomes because the reward is binary: 1.0 only if the entire tool-execution chain succeeds, 0.0 otherwise. This extreme sparsity causes GRPO group variance to collapse (ZVF=1.0) and prevents any learning signal from propagating. We propose a 4-tier dense reward for tool-use tasks: (1) +0.2 for selecting any valid tool name, (2) +0.3 for formatting tool arguments with correct schema, (3) +0.3 for executing the tool without runtime errors, (4) +0.2 for producing the correct final answer. We evaluate this on a simplified tool-use dataset (weather lookup, calculator, web search) using local TRL GRPO with Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct. Metrics: nonzero reward fraction, ZVF, last-10 accuracy, and held-out success rate.
Experiments: Baseline: Binary reward (1.0 for full success, 0.0 otherwise) on 100 tool-use problems with 3 tools. Run GRPO for 50 steps, GROUP_SIZE=8, NUM_SEEDS=3. Record ZVF, nonzero reward fraction, and success rate.
Dense reward: Implement the 4-tier reward described above on the same dataset. Compare ZVF, nonzero reward fraction, and learning curves against baseline.
Ablation: Remove each tier individually to isolate which component carries most of the gradient signal.
Held-out evaluation: Test both reward functions on 50 unseen tool-use problems to verify generalization.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The 4-tier reward requires a tool parser/validator, which may not generalize to arbitrary tool schemas.
Partial credit for argument formatting may reward syntactic correctness over semantic correctness.
Small model (0.5B) may lack the capacity to learn complex tool-use patterns even with dense rewards.
Simplified tool-use dataset may not reflect real-world API complexity.
